title: Pollution Issues Investigation
description: Correcting pollution data with LIKE, UPDATE, and safe modification practices

learning_objectives.description: Apply LIKE operator for pattern matching in text data
Apply UPDATE statements to correct data errors
Create safe data modification workflows using backup tables
Evaluate data integrity by finding contradictory records

content: Did you notice that we recorded contamination/pollution data for all of the well sources? Find the right table and print the first few rows.

tasks.title: Explore the well_pollution table

learning_objectives.description: Understand the well_pollution table and contamination measurements

tasks.content: Let's find and explore the well pollution data.

subtasks.title: Query the well_pollution table
subtasks.description: View the structure and sample data from well_pollution
subtasks.acceptance_criteria:
- Query retrieves first few rows from well_pollution
- Student can identify columns: source_id, date, description, pollutant_ppm, biological, results
- Student understands that biological > 0.01 means contaminated
subtasks.content:
Find the right table and print the first few rows.

**Expected query:**
```sql
SELECT *
FROM well_pollution
```

I get this:

| source_id | date | description | pollutant_ppm | biological | results |
|-----------|------|-------------|---------------|------------|--------|
| KiRu28935224 | 2021-01-04 09:17:00 | Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia | 0.0 | 495.898 | Contaminated: Biological |
| AkLu01628224 | 2021-01-04 09:53:00 | Bacteria: Salmonella Typhi | 0.0 | 376.572 | Contaminated: Biological |
| HaZa21742224 | 2021-01-04 10:37:00 | Inorganic contaminants: Zinc... | 2.715 | 0.0 | Contaminated: Chemical |
| HaRu19725224 | 2021-01-04 11:04:00 | Clean | 0.0288593 | 0.0 | Clean |
| SoRu35703224 | 2021-01-04 11:29:00 | Bacteria: E. coli | 0.0 | 296.437 | Contaminated: Biological |

It looks like our scientists diligently recorded the water quality of all the wells. Some are contaminated with biological contaminants, while others are polluted with an excess of heavy metals and other pollutants. Based on the results, each well was classified as Clean, Contaminated: Biological or Contaminated: Chemical. It is important to know this because wells that are polluted with bio- or other contaminants are not safe to drink. It looks like they recorded the source_id of each test, so we can link it to a source, at some place in Maji Ndogo.

learning_objectives.description: Understand pollution data structure and contamination thresholds

tutor_guidance.teaching_approach: Emphasize the health implications - this data determines if water is safe to drink
hints_to_give: biological column is in CFU/mL - 0 is clean, > 0.01 is contaminated
The results column should match the biological readings

tasks.title: Find data contradictions
tasks.description: Identify wells marked as Clean but with biological contamination

learning_objectives.description: Analyze data for dangerous contradictions

tasks.content:
In the well pollution table, the descriptions are notes taken by our scientists as text, so it will be challenging to process it. The biological column is in units of CFU/mL, so it measures how much contamination is in the water. 0 is clean, and anything more than 0.01 is contaminated.

Let's check the integrity of the data. The worst case is if we have contamination, but we think we don't. People can get sick, so we need to make sure there are no errors here.

subtasks.title: Discuss the health implications of mislabeled wells
subtasks.description: Understand why finding 'Clean' wells with contamination is dangerous
subtasks.content:
Think about the danger here: if a well is marked as 'Clean' in the results column, but actually has biological contamination (biological > 0.01), people will drink contaminated water thinking it's safe.

This is potentially a life-threatening data error. We MUST find and fix these records.

learning_objectives.description: Evaluate the real-world danger of data errors

subtasks.title: Query for contradictory records
subtasks.description: Find wells where results='Clean' but biological > 0.01
dependencies: Discuss the health implications of mislabeled wells
subtasks.acceptance_criteria:
- Query returns rows where results='Clean' AND biological > 0.01
- Uses WHERE with AND to combine both conditions
- Student understands why these records are dangerous
subtasks.content:
So, write a query that checks if the results is Clean but the biological column is > 0.01.

**Expected query:**
```sql
SELECT *
FROM well_pollution
WHERE results = 'Clean'
AND biological > 0.01
```

I got this:

| source_id | date | description | pollutant_ppm | biological | results |
|-----------|------|-------------|---------------|------------|--------|
| AkRu08936224 | 2021-01-08 09:22:00 | Bacteria: E. coli | 0.0406458 | 35.0068 | Clean |
| AkRu06489224 | 2021-01-10 09:44:00 | Clean Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia | 0.0897904 | 38.467 | Clean |
| SoRu38011224 | 2021-01-14 15:35:00 | Bacteria: E. coli | 0.0425095 | 19.2897 | Clean |
| AkKi00955224 | 2021-01-22 12:47:00 | Bacteria: E. coli | 0.0812092 | 40.2273 | Clean |
| KiHa22929224 | 2021-02-06 13:54:00 | Bacteria: E. coli | 0.0722537 | 18.4482 | Clean |
| KiRu25473224 | 2021-02-07 15:51:00 | Clean Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia | 0.0630094 | 24.4536 | Clean |
| HaRu17401224 | 2021-03-01 13:44:00 | Clean Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia | 0.0649209 | 25.8129 | Clean |

If we compare the results of this query to the entire table it seems like we have some inconsistencies in how the well statuses are recorded. Specifically, it seems that some data input personnel might have mistaken the description field for determining the cleanliness of the water.

learning_objectives.description: Analyze data for contradictory values

checks.query: SELECT * FROM well_pollution WHERE results = 'Clean' AND biological > 0.01

tutor_guidance.answer_rationale: If results = 'Clean' the description is saying the well is clean, but AND biological > 0.01 says it is polluted. So this query finds the records that say clean, but are actually dirty.

tasks.title: Use LIKE for pattern matching
tasks.description: Find descriptions with extra 'Clean' prefix using wildcards

learning_objectives.description: Apply LIKE operator with wildcards for pattern matching

tasks.content:
It seems like, in some cases, if the description field begins with the word 'Clean', the results have been classified as 'Clean' in the results column, even though the biological column is > 0.01.

We need to find and remove the 'Clean' part from all the descriptions that do have a biological contamination so this mistake is not made again.

subtasks.title: Understand the error pattern
subtasks.description: Understand why some descriptions have 'Clean' prefix incorrectly
subtasks.content:
Vuyisile has told me that the descriptions should only have the word 'Clean' if there is no biological contamination (and no chemical pollutants). Some data personnel must have copied the data from the scientist's notes into our database incorrectly. We need to find and remove the 'Clean' part from all the descriptions that do have a biological contamination so this mistake is not made again.

First, let's look at the descriptions. We need to identify the records that mistakenly have the word Clean in the description. However, it is important to remember that not all of our field surveyors used the description to set the results - some checked the actual data.

learning_objectives.description: Understand the root cause of data entry errors

subtasks.title: Write LIKE query for wrong descriptions
subtasks.description: Use LIKE with wildcards to find descriptions starting with 'Clean' followed by other text
subtasks.acceptance_criteria:
- Query uses LIKE 'Clean_%' pattern
- Returns 38 wrong descriptions
- Student understands _ matches one character and % matches any characters
subtasks.content:
**Hint:** To find these descriptions, search for the word Clean with additional characters after it. As this is what separates incorrect descriptions from the records that should have 'Clean'.

**Expected query:**
```sql
SELECT *
FROM well_pollution
WHERE description LIKE 'Clean_%'
```

The query should return 38 wrong descriptions.

Looking at the results we can see two different descriptions that we need to fix:
1. All records that mistakenly have 'Clean Bacteria: E. coli' should be updated to 'Bacteria: E. coli'
2. All records that mistakenly have 'Clean Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia' should be updated to 'Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia'

The second issue we need to fix is in our results column. We need to update the results column from 'Clean' to 'Contaminated: Biological' where the biological column has a value greater than 0.01.

learning_objectives.description: Apply LIKE with wildcard patterns

checks.query: SELECT * FROM well_pollution WHERE description LIKE 'Clean_%'

tutor_guidance.answer_rationale: 'Clean_%' means it starts with 'Clean', (_) have at least one character after 'Clean' and (%) can have any other characters after that.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the quotes around the pattern
Using LIKE = instead of just LIKE

tasks.title: Safely update the data
tasks.description: Use a copy table to safely test and apply UPDATE statements

learning_objectives.description: Create safe data modification workflows
Apply UPDATE statements to correct data

tasks.content: Now we need to fix these descriptions so that we don't encounter this issue again in the future. But when we change any data on the database, we need to be SURE there are no errors, as this could fill the database with incorrect values.

subtasks.title: Discuss the responsibility of modifying data
subtasks.description: Understand the risks and best practices for UPDATE statements
subtasks.content:
Before we make any changes, here is another nugget of advice: Begin complex queries by commenting on what you will do. This helps us to think through the problem before we write code to solve it, and once we're done, we have a well-documented code.

Now, when we change any data on the database, we need to be SURE there are no errors, as this could fill the database with incorrect values. A safer way to do the UPDATE is by testing the changes on a copy of the table first.

**Don't worry about breaking things:** If anything goes wrong with your data modifications, you can always reset the database back to its original state. The system can reload a fresh copy of the database for you, so feel free to experiment - that's how you learn! The copy-table approach we're about to use is how professionals do it, but knowing you have a safety net means you can focus on learning without fear.

learning_objectives.description: Understand the importance of safe data modification practices

tutor_guidance.teaching_approach: Emphasize this as a professional best practice that prevents disasters
discussion_prompts: What if your WHERE clause accidentally matched more rows than intended?
How would you undo an UPDATE if you made a mistake?
Why might companies have 'read-only' vs 'read-write' database permissions?

subtasks.title: Create a copy table for safe testing
subtasks.description: Create well_pollution_copy to test UPDATE queries
dependencies: Discuss the responsibility of modifying data
subtasks.acceptance_criteria:
- Copy table well_pollution_copy created successfully
- Contains same data as original well_pollution table
- Student understands why testing on a copy is essential
subtasks.content:
The CREATE TABLE new_table AS query approach is a neat trick that allows you to create a new table from the results set of a query. This method is especially useful for creating backup tables or subsets without the need for a separate CREATE TABLE and INSERT INTO statement.

So if we run:

**Expected query:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE well_pollution_copy AS
    SELECT *
    FROM well_pollution
```

We will get a copy of well_pollution called well_pollution_copy. Now we can make the changes, and if we discover there is a mistake in our code, we can just delete this table, and run it again.

**Tip:** If you ever need to start completely fresh, you can reset the database to its original state. But the copy-table approach is good practice - it's what you'll do in the real world where there is no reset button!

learning_objectives.description: Apply CREATE TABLE AS SELECT to create backup copies

checks.query: CREATE TABLE well_pollution_copy AS SELECT * FROM well_pollution

tutor_guidance.teaching_approach: Emphasize this as a professional best practice
hints_to_give: Always make a copy before modifying production data
Test your UPDATE on the copy first

subtasks.title: Plan the UPDATE with comments first
subtasks.description: Practice the professional habit of planning SQL changes by writing comments before code
dependencies: Create a copy table for safe testing
subtasks.acceptance_criteria:
- Student writes comment outlines before filling in SQL
- Student understands the three cases to fix
- Student can articulate what each UPDATE will do before writing it
subtasks.content:
Before we write the UPDATE queries, here is another nugget of advice: Begin complex queries by commenting on what you will do. This helps us to think through the problem before we write code to solve it, and once we're done, we have a well-documented code.

**Step 1 - Write comments describing what we need to do:**
```sql
-- Case 1a: Update descriptions that mistakenly mention
-- 'Clean Bacteria: E. coli' to 'Bacteria: E. coli'
-- Case 1b: Update the descriptions that mistakenly mention
-- 'Clean Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia' to 'Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia'
-- Case 2: Update the 'results' to 'Contaminated: Biological' where
-- 'biological' is greater than 0.01 plus current results is 'Clean'
```

**Step 2 - Add the SQL structure with comments as guides:**
```sql
-- Case 1a
UPDATE
   -- Update well_pollution_copy table
SET
   -- Change description to 'Bacteria: E. coli'
WHERE
   -- Where the description is 'Clean Bacteria: E. coli'

-- Case 1b
   -- Try to fill this in
-- Case 2
   -- Try to fill this in
```

**Step 3 - Fill in the actual SQL:**
```sql
-- Case 1a
UPDATE
   well_pollution_copy
SET
   description = 'Bacteria: E. coli'
WHERE
   description = 'Clean Bacteria: E. coli';

-- Case 1b
   -- Try to fill this in
-- Case 2
   -- Try to fill this in
```

Ok, go ahead and fill in the rest.

learning_objectives.description: Apply the practice of planning SQL modifications with comments before writing code

tutor_guidance.teaching_approach: Walk through the progression from comments to code - this is a professional habit that prevents mistakes on production data
discussion_prompts: Why is it helpful to write comments before the actual SQL?
How does this approach reduce errors?
hints_to_give: Case 1b follows the same pattern as 1a but for Giardia Lamblia
Case 2 needs to check both the biological value AND the current results

subtasks.title: Write UPDATE queries for the copy table
subtasks.description: Write and test UPDATE queries on the copy table
dependencies: Plan the UPDATE with comments first
subtasks.acceptance_criteria:
- All three UPDATE queries written correctly
- Updates tested on well_pollution_copy first
- Student can explain what each UPDATE does
subtasks.content:
So if we now run our query on the copy table:

**Case 1a - Fix E. coli descriptions:**
```sql
UPDATE well_pollution_copy
SET description = 'Bacteria: E. coli'
WHERE description = 'Clean Bacteria: E. coli'
```

**Case 1b - Fix Giardia descriptions:**
```sql
UPDATE well_pollution_copy
SET description = 'Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia'
WHERE description = 'Clean Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia'
```

**Case 2 - Fix results column:**
```sql
UPDATE well_pollution_copy
SET results = 'Contaminated: Biological'
WHERE biological > 0.01 AND results = 'Clean'
```

learning_objectives.description: Create UPDATE statements with WHERE conditions

checks.query: UPDATE well_pollution_copy SET description = 'Bacteria: E. coli' WHERE description = 'Clean Bacteria: E. coli'
UPDATE well_pollution_copy SET description = 'Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia' WHERE description = 'Clean Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia'
UPDATE well_pollution_copy SET results = 'Contaminated: Biological' WHERE biological > 0.01 AND results = 'Clean'

tutor_guidance.answer_rationale:
Case 1a and 1b fix the description field by removing the erroneous 'Clean ' prefix. Case 2 fixes the results column by setting it to 'Contaminated: Biological' where the data shows contamination but was incorrectly marked as Clean.

**Advanced approach (MEMO):** For stronger students, show that Cases 1a/1b and Case 2 can be combined into single UPDATE statements that fix both columns at once:
```sql
UPDATE well_pollution_copy
SET results = 'Contaminated: Biological',
    description = 'Bacteria: E. coli'
WHERE description = 'Clean Bacteria: E. coli';

UPDATE well_pollution_copy
SET results = 'Contaminated: Biological',
    description = 'Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia'
WHERE description = 'Clean Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia';
```
This is more efficient because it updates both the description and results in one pass, rather than requiring a separate Case 2 UPDATE.

subtasks.title: Verify the fixes worked
subtasks.description: Run a verification query to confirm all errors are fixed
dependencies: Write UPDATE queries for the copy table
subtasks.acceptance_criteria:
- Verification query returns 0 rows
- Student understands how to verify data corrections
subtasks.content:
We can then check if our errors are fixed using a SELECT query on the well_pollution_copy table:

**Expected query:**
```sql
SELECT *
FROM well_pollution_copy
WHERE description LIKE 'Clean_%'
OR (results = 'Clean' AND biological > 0.01)
```

This should return 0 rows if all our fixes worked correctly.

checks.query: SELECT * FROM well_pollution_copy WHERE description LIKE 'Clean_%' OR (results = 'Clean' AND biological > 0.01)

learning_objectives.description: Evaluate the success of data corrections

subtasks.title: Apply updates to production and cleanup
subtasks.description: Apply the verified updates to the real table and drop the copy
subtasks.acceptance_criteria:
- Updates applied to well_pollution table
- Copy table dropped with DROP TABLE
- Student understands the complete safe modification workflow
subtasks.content:
Then if we're sure it works as intended, we can change the table back to the well_pollution and delete the well_pollution_copy table.

```sql
UPDATE well_pollution
SET description = 'Bacteria: E. coli'
WHERE description = 'Clean Bacteria: E. coli';

UPDATE well_pollution
SET description = 'Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia'
WHERE description = 'Clean Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia';

UPDATE well_pollution
SET results = 'Contaminated: Biological'
WHERE biological > 0.01 AND results = 'Clean';

DROP TABLE well_pollution_copy;
```

**Remember:** These changes modify the real `well_pollution` table. If something doesn't look right after running these, you can reset the database to its original state and try again from scratch. In the real world you wouldn't have this luxury, which is exactly why we practised on a copy first!

So I hope that today helped you to see what the survey data is all about. I hope that you are as excited as I am to get stuck in! Until then, keep well!

learning_objectives.description: Apply DROP TABLE to clean up temporary tables

checks.query: UPDATE well_pollution SET description = 'Bacteria: E. coli' WHERE description = 'Clean Bacteria: E. coli'
UPDATE well_pollution SET description = 'Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia' WHERE description = 'Clean Bacteria: Giardia Lamblia'
UPDATE well_pollution SET results = 'Contaminated: Biological' WHERE biological > 0.01 AND results = 'Clean'

tutor_guidance.teaching_approach: End on a high note - connect their work to meaningful outcomes
discussion_prompts: How has your view of 'data analysis' changed?
What would you do differently if you encountered messy data again?
Why does careful, verified work matter more than fast work?
